The present survey of Vergilian scholarship is the twentieth in the Vergilius series. Classical
World 68:1
(1974), published in association with the
Vergilian
Society,
Inc.,
contains
my 'Recent Work on Vergil: A Bibliographical Survey, 1964-73/ The survey was reprinted in The Classical World
Bibliography of Vergil (Walter Donlan, ed.), Garland Publishing, Inc. (New
York and London 1978). Subsequent reviews have appeared in Vergilius 19
(1973), 20 (1974), 21 (1975), 22 (1976), 23 (1977), 24 (1978), 25 (1979),
26 (1980), and 27 (1981).
The Vergilian Bimillenium (19 B.C. - A.D. 1982) has excited scholars to
renewed enthusiasm for the Master Poet. Special symposia and colloquia have
celebrated the event
in Europe, the United Kingdom, and North America
with generous outpourings of articles and addresses in their train. We await
the publication of proceedings of several gatherings in Mainz, Prague, Mantua,
Rome, Naples, and Cumae (The Vergilian Society's event).
The present compilation owes much to the assistance and cooperation of
the Mills Memorial Library of McMaster University, to the Robarts Library of
the University of Toronto, to the library of the American Academy in Rome,
and to the Carolinum
Library
in
Prague. Offprints of articles and bibliographic notices for inclusion in the annual Vergilius review are welcomed with
enthusiasm and scholars are asked to send material to the editor of Vergilian
Bibliography, c/o Department of Classics, McMaster University, Hamilton,
Ontario, Canada L8S 4M2.

66
with careful criticism of past exercises. Chevalliers volume (2) contains a
varied repertoire (abbrev. Colloque l'épopée),
as does Giganteas well organized collection of essays on the Eclogues (abbrev.: Gigante). The Cambridge
History of Classical Literature contains essays by W. V. Clausen (Theocritus
and Virgil), L. P. Wilkinson (The Georgics), and R. D. Williams (The Aeneid).
Suerbaum's reflections on recent studies of the epic are an outgrowth of his
monumental bibliographical study. Delia Corte's protean Enciclopedia Virgiliana has published a sample fascicle which serves as splendid prelude to a
promising enterprise, as meaningful and exciting as Lexicon Iconographicum
Mythoiogiae Ciassicae, another goldmine of fact and bibliography.

Eclogues and Georgics continues to find favor with scholars who regret the
constricted format but respect the views of an experienced commentator.

raw_text:
Otto Skutsch, 'Numbers in Virgil's Bucolics/ BICS 27 (1980) 95-6.
Conte, inter alia, examines programmatic proems at midpoint in Vergil's
several works. The Danaid myth on Pallas' swordbelt {Aen. 10.496ff) Conte
regards as accenting the pathos of Pallas' missed marriage; and the Helen
episode, modelled after Achilles' quarrel with Agamemnon (Iliad 1), features
Venus as Athena's counterpart, Conte argues, and so supports authenticity
(pace Goold, HSCP 74 [1970J101-68). Horsfall 's article refers to both Eclogues and Georgics (108-9). Segal's essays are reprints of earlier offerings.
Otto Skutsch responds to David West (CR 28 [1978] 77f) and continues to
detect arithmetical structure in the Eclogue-Book.

raw_text:
Stephanie West, 'Some watchers of the skies/ CQ 32 (1982) 121-3.
Brenk refers to Diotimos (A.P. 7.261) and Homer (Iliad 11.91 -100) in his
interpretation of the complex, mysterious Bianor in Eclogue 9. Campbell's
solution of the riddle of Damoetas is persuasive. Keppie's study of the confiscation suggests that colonists at Cremona may have included members of
Caesar's old Legio X, along with veterans from another legion, maybe Legio 11
or (more likely)
Legio XV. Certainly the settlers on Vergil's terrain were
neither barbarous mercenaries nor foreign mercenaries. Veyne treats Tityrus'
peculium and Eclogues I and IX. Stephanie West examines Eclogues 3.40-42
and Aen. 6.849-50 and identifies radius (from analogies in Avienus, Arat,
53-4, and Catullus 66.1 ff.) as a surveying instrument.

raw_text:
Joel Thomas, Structures de l'imaginaire dans l'Énéide (Paris 1981 ).
81.
R. D. Williams, 'Virgil's Aeneid - The First 2,000 Years,' PCA 78
(1981) 7-16.
Bandera notices that hardly any violent death in the epic lacks some
sacrificial overtones, some echo of the unum pro multis formula. Creusa,
Orontes, and Palinurus are sacrificial prelude to the symbolic descent of
Aeneas and to the awful violence ahead on Italian soil. Briggs studies Vergil's
design in employing repetitions to unite the Georgics and Aeneid by themes
and language. Dirckx deals with Aeneid 3.137-42 and Georgics 2.151-2, and
Aeneid 12.383-429
in
his medical study. Griffin attacks the validity of a
typological approach to the Aeneid. Holt treats dual perspective as a pervasive
feature of the epic. Rudd's memoir of T. S. Page, the Macmillan editor,
scholar, and controversialist, includes a comparison of Page's edition of Vergil
and Horace with those of forerunners and successors. Suerbaum's assessment
of Vergil's place in the classroom and seminar includes studies of the end of
the epic (46-103), and of the contest of Aeneas and Turnus and readers'
reactions (104-125); a select bibliography
is included (144-147). Deryck
Williams' presidential address to the British Classical Association considers the
multiplicity of Vergil's poetic abilities, surface and underlying qualities, the
sound factor and descriptive power of Vergil, his high-powered and intense
rhetoric, along with inner, deeper qualities of the poem.

raw_text:
Joel Thomas, Structures de l'imaginaire dans l'Énéide (Paris 1981 ).
81.
R. D. Williams, 'Virgil's Aeneid - The First 2,000 Years,' PCA 78
(1981) 7-16.
Bandera notices that hardly any violent death in the epic lacks some
sacrificial overtones, some echo of the unum pro multis formula. Creusa,
Orontes, and Palinurus are sacrificial prelude to the symbolic descent of
Aeneas and to the awful violence ahead on Italian soil. Briggs studies Vergil's
design in employing repetitions to unite the Georgics and Aeneid by themes
and language. Dirckx deals with Aeneid 3.137-42 and Georgics 2.151-2, and
Aeneid 12.383-429
in
his medical study. Griffin attacks the validity of a
typological approach to the Aeneid. Holt treats dual perspective as a pervasive
feature of the epic. Rudd's memoir of T. S. Page, the Macmillan editor,
scholar, and controversialist, includes a comparison of Page's edition of Vergil
and Horace with those of forerunners and successors. Suerbaum's assessment
of Vergil's place in the classroom and seminar includes studies of the end of
the epic (46-103), and of the contest of Aeneas and Turnus and readers'
reactions (104-125); a select bibliography
is included (144-147). Deryck
Williams' presidential address to the British Classical Association considers the
multiplicity of Vergil's poetic abilities, surface and underlying qualities, the
sound factor and descriptive power of Vergil, his high-powered and intense
rhetoric, along with inner, deeper qualities of the poem.

raw_text:
G.
Lenoir, 'L'aristie d'Énée au livre XII {'Enéide, XII, 505-567),'
Colloque l'épopée 93-99.
De Grummond examines Vergil's feelings at the close of the epic in light
of Juno's feelings at the outset. Juno's enduring, destructive wrath and resentment, born of hatred, and self-love, initiate the poem's movement. Aeneas'
quick, accelerating wrath, and resentment, born of his love for others, terminates the action; his acceptance of necessity leads to the resolution, and to
general prosperity. Kinsey interprets Troianus. . . Caesar (1.286) as implying
Augustus; and argues that Sidon was the dominant partner (with Tyre) for
Vergil. Segal's examination of lopas' song responds to Kinsey (Emerita 47,
1979, 77-86) and argues that 'symbolic' meaning should not be conceived too
narrowly; neither literalism, nor allegory, is a valid approach to lopas' song.
Cleary
offers exciting suggestions
for a sound and slide presentation of
Aeneid 2
in conjunction
with Picasso's masterpiece.
Edgeworth examines
colour terms. Bright studies the sequence of events and patterns of Odyssey
9-12 and Aeneid 3. The visit to Buthrotum claims major notice as a nekyia
(Helenus = Teiresias)
in its own right. Estevez argues that Jupiters reaction
(Aeneid 4.220)
is marked
by hasty intemperance and a fall
from grace.
Jocelyn studies the text and interprets Macrobius Sat. 3.2.7-9. Kinsey (91)
studies Dido's references to fides and dextra (Aen. 4.307, 314, 597) and
detaches them from any allusion to marriage. They refer tç the obligations
Aeneas contracted when he accepted Dido's hospitality. Houston studies Vergil's account of the Lusus Troiae and Augustan policy. Tarrant examines the
Platonic factor in the Gates of Sleep. Yardley detects a sarcastic allusion to
the komos
in Menelaus amans (Aen. 6.523-6); Deiphobus casts Menelaus in
the role of admissus amans and himself as cuckolded husband; hence iimini
(526) belongs not to the thalamus of Deiphobus and Helen but to the house.
Falkner shows how parallels between the vision of Hector and the Deiphobus
episode enable Vergil to highlight fundamental differences between Aeneid 2
and 8. The second interview assures Aeneas that he has rendered full duty to
his fatherland, and that to have done more was impossible. Johnston examines the tidal wave structure of Aeneid 1 and detects a sequence whereby a
peaceful people is converted into a race of warriors. The pattern drawn from
the physical world may be viewed as an exempium in parvo of the tragic,
metaphysical storm which rises out oí Aeneid VII.' (29). Glazewski treats the
catalogue in Aeneid 7; Rowland explores catalogues and lists in Aeneid 1 and

Aen. 10, 362-79; Livius 22, 50, 4-1 2)/ AU 2 (1980) 13-26.

title: G. Lenoir, 'L'aristie d'Énée au livre XII {'Enéide, XII, 505-567),' Colloque l'épopée 93-99. De Grummond examines Vergil's feelings at the close of the epic in light of Juno's feelings at the outset. Juno's enduring, destructive wrath and resentment, born of hatred, and self-love, initiate the poem's movement. Aeneas' quick, accelerating wrath, and resentment, born of his love for others, terminates the action; his acceptance of necessity leads to the resolution, and to general prosperity. Kinsey interprets Troianus. . . Caesar (1.286) as implying Augustus; and argues that Sidon was the dominant partner (with Tyre) for Vergil. Segal's examination of lopas' song responds to Kinsey (Emerita 47, 1979, 77-86) and argues that 'symbolic' meaning should not be conceived too narrowly; neither literalism, nor allegory, is a valid approach to lopas' song. Cleary offers exciting suggestions for a sound and slide presentation of Aeneid 2 in conjunction with Picasso's masterpiece. Edgeworth examines colour terms. Bright studies the sequence of events and patterns of Odyssey 9-12 and Aeneid 3. The visit to Buthrotum claims major notice as a nekyia (Helenus = Teiresias) in its own right. Estevez argues that Jupiters reaction (Aeneid 4.220) is marked by hasty intemperance and a fall from grace. Jocelyn studies the text and interprets Macrobius Sat. 3.2.7-9. Kinsey (91) studies Dido's references to fides and dextra (Aen. 4.307, 314, 597) and detaches them from any allusion to marriage. They refer tç the obligations Aeneas contracted when he accepted Dido's hospitality. Houston studies Vergil's account of the Lusus Troiae and Augustan policy. Tarrant examines the Platonic factor in the Gates of Sleep. Yardley detects a sarcastic allusion to the komos in Menelaus amans (Aen. 6.523-6); Deiphobus casts Menelaus in the role of admissus amans and himself as cuckolded husband; hence iimini (526) belongs not to the thalamus of Deiphobus and Helen but to the house. Falkner shows how parallels between the vision of Hector and the Deiphobus episode enable Vergil to highlight fundamental differences between Aeneid 2 and 8. The second interview assures Aeneas that he has rendered full duty to his fatherland, and that to have done more was impossible. Johnston examines the tidal wave structure of Aeneid 1 and detects a sequence whereby a peaceful people is converted into a race of warriors. The pattern drawn from the physical world may be viewed as an exempium in parvo of the tragic, metaphysical storm which rises out oí Aeneid VII.' (29). Baker's chapter is an incisive, helpful estimate of Ascanius' character with

insights. Burnell finds that brave men of Roman mythology tend to

77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:12:47 UTC
74
Aeneas. The mutilated figure illustrates a crisis in the traditional heroic code
and underscores the need to transcend it in the future. La Penna's critical
review of Mezentius studies is valuable. Lee, recently concerned with fatherson relationships in the epic, embraces Palinurus and lapyx, both brothers of
lasus, in a sympathetic study. Perkell is concerned with Dido's faulty commitment to a traditional Roman, and intractable ideal {pietas); her career is
marked
by a
conflict of heroic aspiration with humanity. Wiltshire and
Krickel examine Diomedes as a double for Aeneas, helping in the redefinition
of heroism in Roman terms. The defeated Diomedes serves also as a model for
benevolent, peaceful rulers of the sort that Aeneas and Augustus are meant to be.
126.

raw_text:
Virgilio illustrato nel libro (Secc. IV-XIX) [supra]. Catalogue, with
plates. In press. (Biblioteca apostolica Vaticana, Servizio delle Pubblicazioni, I
-00120, Città del Vaticano).
The spectacular exhibition, Enea nel Lazio, produced a valuable catalogue
and commentary on contentious issues pertaining to Vergil's Latium. Ingrid
Edlunďs article is required reading along with the catalogue. Goodfellow's
essay on topographical (and poetical) features of the Georgics as outgrowth
of Vergil's north
Italian experience enriches understanding of the poems.
Jucker's illustrated article on Apollo Palatinus and Apollo Actius illuminates
Vergil's references to
the Palatine temple and the Actian memorials. The
marvelously illustrated articles relevant to Vergilian characters and sites in
Volume I of Lexicon Iconographicum Mythologiae Classicae are authoritative
and presage better things to come. Thomas' book makes available the content
and argument of an earlier doctoral study. The Vatican exhibition of Vergilian books, mounted in connection with the bimillenary celebration, claims
74
Aeneas. The mutilated figure illustrates a crisis in the traditional heroic code
and underscores the need to transcend it in the future. La Penna's critical
review of Mezentius studies is valuable. Lee, recently concerned with fatherson relationships in the epic, embraces Palinurus and lapyx, both brothers of
lasus, in a sympathetic study. Perkell is concerned with Dido's faulty commitment to a traditional Roman, and intractable ideal {pietas); her career is
marked
by a
conflict of heroic aspiration with humanity. Wiltshire and
Krickel examine Diomedes as a double for Aeneas, helping in the redefinition
of heroism in Roman terms. The defeated Diomedes serves also as a model for
benevolent, peaceful rulers of the sort that Aeneas and Augustus are meant to be.
126.
Ingrid E. M. Edlund, 'The archaeology of Rome and Latium in
Vergil's Aeneid/ Vergilius 27 (1981) 1-7.

Edlunďs article is required reading along with the catalogue. Goodfellow's
essay on topographical (and poetical) features of the Georgics as outgrowth
of Vergil's north
Italian experience enriches understanding of the poems.
Jucker's illustrated article on Apollo Palatinus and Apollo Actius illuminates
Vergil's references to
the Palatine temple and the Actian memorials. The
marvelously illustrated articles relevant to Vergilian characters and sites in
Volume I of Lexicon Iconographicum Mythologiae Classicae are authoritative
and presage better things to come. Thomas' book makes available the content
and argument of an earlier doctoral study. The Vatican exhibition of Vergilian books, mounted in connection with the bimillenary celebration, claims
74
Aeneas. The mutilated figure illustrates a crisis in the traditional heroic code
and underscores the need to transcend it in the future. La Penna's critical
review of Mezentius studies is valuable. Lee, recently concerned with fatherson relationships in the epic, embraces Palinurus and lapyx, both brothers of
lasus, in a sympathetic study. Perkell is concerned with Dido's faulty commitment to a traditional Roman, and intractable ideal {pietas); her career is
marked
by a
conflict of heroic aspiration with humanity. Wiltshire and
Krickel examine Diomedes as a double for Aeneas, helping in the redefinition
of heroism in Roman terms. The defeated Diomedes serves also as a model for
benevolent, peaceful rulers of the sort that Aeneas and Augustus are meant to be.
126.

raw_text:
A. Thornton, The Living Universe: Gods and Men in Virgil's Aeneid
(London 1977). Rev: A. Wlosok, Gnomon 53 (1981) 751-62.
Vergil and his time turned the term, Golden Age, inside out, according to
Brenk. Eclogue IV reveals elements radically opposed to the Hesiodic concept; gold
is an ambiguous symbol
in
the epic; and
the Golden
Age, as
imposition of law and order on a somewhat recalcitrant world, gets only
half-hearted support.
Jacobson shows that Vergil has transformed a traditional hypothetical argument in philosophical discourse into a primeval historical event. Rabel views the top (Aen. 7.378-87) through Stoic eyes where
Allecto is merely the proximate auxiliary cause of Amata's anger, the beginning of motion (acc. to Cicero's De Fato);otherwise she moves inaccord with
the force of her own bitter, angry nature.

raw_text:
A. Thornton, The Living Universe: Gods and Men in Virgil's Aeneid
(London 1977). Rev: A. Wlosok, Gnomon 53 (1981) 751-62.
Vergil and his time turned the term, Golden Age, inside out, according to
Brenk. Eclogue IV reveals elements radically opposed to the Hesiodic concept; gold
is an ambiguous symbol
in
the epic; and
the Golden
Age, as
imposition of law and order on a somewhat recalcitrant world, gets only
half-hearted support.
Jacobson shows that Vergil has transformed a traditional hypothetical argument in philosophical discourse into a primeval historical event. Rabel views the top (Aen. 7.378-87) through Stoic eyes where
Allecto is merely the proximate auxiliary cause of Amata's anger, the beginning of motion (acc. to Cicero's De Fato);otherwise she moves inaccord with
the force of her own bitter, angry nature.

raw_text:
J.Van Sickle, 'Poetics of opening and closure in Meleager, Catullus,
and Gallus,' CW 75 (1981) 65-75.
Gallus and the new fragment captures the limelight again. D'Anna examines Eclogue 6.64-73, and 10.50-60. Graf, with Van Sickle, provides useful
commentary and bibliography on
work
to date on the Gallus verses. Lee
responds to Roland Meyer's disbelief with reference to Augustine, Confessions 6.6.9, relating to the syntax of Gallus. The contest is concerned with
tu . . . pars eritt or eris.

raw_text:
R. D. Williams, 'Virgil, Aen. 6.304,' CQ 31 (1981) 469-70.
Pope adopts Janell's approach
to Aen. 4.311, and admits no pause or
interrogation after quid for reasons of sense and rhythm. Skutsch argues that
commas should be placed around sed proximus {Aen. 5.320)
in order to
improve the sense. Skutsch explains the subjunctive
in Aen. 6.394. Timpanaro offers an acute study of an oft-emended sentence of the Danieline
scholium on Aen. 4.219 and offers a solution. Williams argues for retaining
deo against Martin West's adeo (contained in a note on Hesiod, Works and
Days, 705).

raw_text:
Paola Venini, 'Nota virgiliana (Aen. 1.81-86),' Athenaeum 59 (1981)
489.
Boella studies Vergil and Catullus 101 (324-6), and Vergil, Aen. 12.879-84
and Bion of Smyrna (326-8). Harrison underscores Vergil's originality in imitation accenting his deviations from Homeric models for striking effects,
including oppositio.
Vergil,
the spokesman of the Aeneadae, corrects the
tradition. Paratore studies Aeneid 9-12 in his review of war episodes in both
poets. Venini aligns Iliad 23.21 2-21 6 with Aeneid 1.81-6.

raw_text:
Rick M. Newton, 'Trimalchio's Hellish Bath,' CJ 77 (1982) 315-9.
Polybius,
freedman
of Claudius,
produced a Greek
Vergil
(probably
Aeneid)-,
Arrian, the epic poet, translated the Georglcs into Greek hexameters; Constantine used a hexameter rendering of Eclogue 4. Baldwin's article
adduces the evidence of extant papyrus texts to indicate that the poet's fame
persisted in allusions into the mid-1 5th century. Horsfall notes a thematic
reworking of Aeneid 1.34ff in Apuleius. Newton's article treats the catabasis
motif in Satyricon 72 and suggests that Petronius intended the scene in
Trimalchio's bath to recall Vergil's vision of the Elysian Fields. The comic
inversion and incongruity are assessed: Encolpius, as an anti-Aeneas, Trimalchio as an unlikely Orpheus, and the guests, as heroes who fell at Troy.

raw_text:
H. Naumann, 'Was wissen wir von Vergils Leben?,' AU 24:5 (1981)
5-16.
Naumann, in both English and German, argues that the Vergilian Vita of
Donatus' commentary is the Life composed by Suetonius, and adduces facts
and other considerations to support the verdict. Lipking's venturesome but
uneven study is concerned with the life and personality of poets, including
Vergil,
with
the biography that invades
their poems.
But there are some
disturbing arguments: 'the death of Misenus stands for his city's cursed past
which must be buried before the survivors can be reborn;' and Turnus is
characterized as a 'stone-age savage' in a strained interpretation of Aeneid
12.896-8.
78
178.
Barry Baldwin, 'Vergil
in Byzantium,' Antike und Abendland 28
(1982) 81-93.

raw_text:
H. Naumann, 'Was wissen wir von Vergils Leben?,' AU 24:5 (1981)
5-16.
Naumann, in both English and German, argues that the Vergilian Vita of
Donatus' commentary is the Life composed by Suetonius, and adduces facts
and other considerations to support the verdict. Lipking's venturesome but
uneven study is concerned with the life and personality of poets, including
Vergil,
with
the biography that invades
their poems.
But there are some
disturbing arguments: 'the death of Misenus stands for his city's cursed past
which must be buried before the survivors can be reborn;' and Turnus is
characterized as a 'stone-age savage' in a strained interpretation of Aeneid
12.896-8.

raw_text:
J.Rathofer, 'Litterae Vergilianae. Zur Farbbeilage aus dem Vergilius
Vaticanus,' Eos 2 (1918) 25-6.
Gaebel shows that the relationship of two manuscripts, Vatican Palatinus
lat. 1631 (P) and Guelferbytanus Gudianus lat. 2° 70 (gamma) is much less
close than has been assumed by Ribbeck, Sabbadini, Mynors, and Geymonat.
Gaebel examines four manuscripts for Bucolics 1-3, including Vat. lat. 3867
(R) of the fourth century, and Bernensis 172(a) of the ninth century. Georgics 4.1-225
is
included
in the examination
with
important conclusions.
Courtney's article, after examining numerous instances of textual problems
remarks that there is a need for frank acknowledgment of the existence of
genuine difficulties in Vergil's text which require close study and a search for
answers. 'These solutions . . . have in some cases come from scholars, such as
Peerlkamp, whose names are hardly vogue words; but the fact that such
scholars have made dozens of unwarranted suggestions does not absolve us
from impartial consideration and recognition of their successes' (28).

raw_text:
Joachim Draheim, Vertonungen antiker Texte vom Barock bis zur
Gegenwart (Heuremata, Studien zur Literatur, Sprachen und Kultur der Antike, Band 7) [Amsterdam 1981].
209.
R. D. Williams, T. S. Pattie,
Virgil: His Poetry through the Ages
(London 1982)
[in connection with the British Library Exhibition, 17 September, 1982- 27 February, 1983].
The exhibition catalogue (198) illustrates Vergil's role in European art and
cultural history: La fortuna di Virgilio attraverso le opere a stampa (9-24); La
fortuna di Virgilio nella cultura Europea (25-62); La fortuna di Virgilio ne/I'
arte (63-262), with lavish illustrations; and Bibliography (263-8). The catalogue provides splendid support for studies in Vergilian influence in several
realms. Kaster studies Vergilian references in Macrobius' Saturnalia. Perkins
and Reeves shed fresh light on T. S. Eliot's ties with Vergil. Draheim's invaluable study of Vergilian influence on musical compositions from the Baroque
period
to
modern
times
lists
works
by
Benjamin
Britten, CastelnuovoTedesco, Charles
Ives, Malipiero, Penderecki, Stravinsky,
Richard Strauss,
Liszt,
the
incomparable
Mozart,
Schubert,
and
others (30-32; 220-225;
261-2).
R. D. Williams and T. S. Pattie (Assistant Keeper, Department of
Manuscripts of the British Library) have collaborated to produce a critical
reassessment of Vergil's poetry, with studies of the manuscript tradition, and
of the immense influence of his poetry on succeeding generations of Latin
and English poets, and on the popular imagination. Williams provides Part I:
Virgil and his poetry; the poems; and Virgil today. A selective bibliography
appears in the volume and appendices include details of Latin mss of Vergil in
the British Library.
McMaster University
21 September, 1982
Alexander G. McKay
80

Pattie (Assistant Keeper, Department of Manuscripts of the British Library) have collaborated to produce a critical reassessment of Vergil's poetry, with studies of the manuscript tradition, and of the immense influence of his poetry on succeeding generations of Latin and English poets, and on the popular imagination. Williams provides Part I: Virgil and his poetry; the poems; and Virgil today. A selective bibliography appears in the volume and appendices include details of Latin mss of Vergil in the British Library. McMaster University 21 September, 1982 Alexander G.
